2. An Optimization ProblemLet AA be an m×nm×n matrix and let rank(A)=krank(A)=k. Suppose, among all the unit vectors in RnRn, we want to find that vector x∈Rnx^∈Rn that maximizes ||Ax||2||Ax||2. Formally,x=
argmax

The rank of ATAATA is rr. We can repeat the above steps again and again for kk times. Summary• Among all the unit vectors in RnRn, x=v1x=v1, which is the eigenvector of ATAATA corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of ATAATA, maximizes ||Ax||2||Ax||2• Among all the unit vectors in RnRn that are orthogonal to v1v1, x=v2x=v2, which is the eigenvector of ATAATA corresponding to the 2nd2nd largest eigenvalue of ATAATA, maximizes ||Ax||2||Ax||2• Among all the unit vectors in RnRn that are orthogonal to {v1,...,vk-1}{v1,...,vk-1}, x=vkx=vk, which is the eigenvector of ATAATA corresponding to the kthkth largest eigenvalue of ATAATA, maximizes ||Ax||2||Ax||2
3. Covariance Matrix{x1,...,xn}{x1,...,xn} are nn data points in RdRd, represented as column vectors. The mean of these data points in 00. Consider matrix XX.X=a

f1f2⋯fd

X=[―x1T――x2T―⋮―xnT―],X=[|||f1f2⋯fd|||]Each row in XX is a data point and each column in XX is a feature vector. The data points are elements in RdRd, therefore, there are dd feature vectors. The matrix C=1
nn∑i=1xixTi=1
nXTXC=1n∑i=1nxixiT=1nXTX is called the covariance matrix. In general, for data points {x1,...,xn}{x1,...,xn}, with ⏨xx¯ as the mean of the data points, the covariance matrix, is given byC=1
nn∑i=1(xi-⏨x)(xi-⏨x)TC=1n∑i=1n(xi-x¯)(xi-x¯)TAs we have mentioned earlier, the mean of our dataset is 00, therefore, C=1

C=1nXTX=1n[―f1T――f2T―⋮―fdT―][|||f1f2⋯fd|||]=1n[f1Tf1f1Tf2⋯f1Tfdf2Tf1f2Tf2⋯f2Tfd⋮⋮⋱⋮fdTf1fdTf2⋯fdTfd]=[Var(f1)Cov(f1,f2)⋯Cov(f1,fd)Cov(f2,f1)Var(f2)⋯Cov(f2,fd)⋮⋮⋱⋮Cov(fd,f1)Cov(fd,f2)⋯Var(fd)] The element in the ithith row and jthjth column is the covariance of feature fifi and feature fjfj.Cov(fi,fj)=fTifj
nCov(fi,fj)=fiTfjnIf i=ji=j, then it becomes the variance of feature fifi. Properties• CC is a symmetric matrix.• CC is a positive semi-definite matrix. That is, all the eigenvalues of CC are grater than or equal to 00.• CC is orthogonally diagonalizable
4. PCA MotivationConsider the following dataset. The mean of this data set is 00.D={x1,...,xn}, D⊂RdD={x1,...,xn},D⊂Rd How many real numbers do we need to store the dataset? We have nn data points in RdRd, therefore, we need n×dn×d real numbers to store the dataset. Can we do better? Yes, if DD lies in a kk dimensional subspace in RdRd, k<dk<d. Suppose, the following set is a basis set for the kk dimensional subspace which contains the dataset.B={𝛽1,...,𝛽k}, B⊂RdB={𝛽1,...,𝛽k},B⊂Rd Each data point xixi is a linear combination of the vectors in BB. Here, a(i,j)a(i,j) is the coefficient of 𝛽j𝛽j in the expansion of xixi.xi=𝛼(i,1)𝛽1+𝛼(i,2)𝛽2+...+𝛼(i,k)𝛽kxi=𝛼(i,1)𝛽1+𝛼(i,2)𝛽2+...+𝛼(i,k)𝛽k For each data point, we need kk scalars. For nn data points, we need n×kn×k scalars. Also, we need to store the vectors in BB. There are kk data points in BB. We need k×dk×d real numbers. Overall, we need to store (n×k)+(k×d)=k(n+d)(n×k)+(k×d)=k(n+d) real numbers. ExampleSuppose we have 10001000 data points in R100R100. We would have to store 100000100000 numbers. If the data points lie in k=10k=10 dimensional subspace in R100R100, then
k(n+d)=10(1000+100)
=11000
k(n+d)=10(1000+100)=11000we can store 1100011000 real numbers. Choice for the BasisTo reduce the number of real numbers required to store the dataset, we can technically choose any basis set which spans the kk dimensional subspace in RdRd containing the dataset. Conside the following.X=a
―xT1―
―xT2―
⋮
―xTn―
X=[―x1T――x2T―⋮―xnT―] Let C=1
nXTXC=1nXTX be the covariance matrix. The data points lie in a kk dimensional subspace in RdRd. Therefore, rank(X)=krank(X)=k. Also, rank(C)=krank(C)=k We can find kk eigenvalues for CC that are greater than 00. They are::𝜆1⩾𝜆2⩾...𝜆k>0𝜆1⩾𝜆2⩾...𝜆k>0 Let vivi be the eigenvector of CC corresoponding to 𝜆i𝜆i and let ||vi||=1||vi||=1. Consider the following set{v1,...,vk}{v1,...,vk} Since CC is a symmetric matrix, we can choose the above set as an orthonormal set. Claim : D=Span({v1,...,vk})D=Span({v1,...,vk}). Proof :
Cvi=𝜆ivi
1
nXTXvi		=𝜆ivi
XTXvi=n𝜆ivi
Cvi=𝜆ivi1nXTXvi=𝜆iviXTXvi=n𝜆ivi Here, XviXvi is a column vector multiplying XTXT to get n𝜆ivin𝜆ivi. When we multiply a matrix with a column vector, the resultant is a linear combination of the columns of the matrix. Therefore, n𝜆ivin𝜆ivi is a linear combination of the columns of XTXT. n𝜆ivin𝜆ivi is in the column space of XTXT. If a vector is an element of a vector space, then any scalar multiple of that vector is also an element of the vector space. Therefore, vivi is an element of the column space of XTXT. The set {v1,...,vk}{v1,...,vk} is contianed in the column space of XTXT. Every column in XTXT is a data point. Which means, the set {v1,...,vk}{v1,...,vk} is contained in the vector space spanned by all the data points in DD. The dataset is contained in a kk dimensional subspace of RdRd. Similarly, span({v1,..,vk})span({v1,..,vk}) is also a kk dimensional subspace in RdRd. The {v1,...,vn}{v1,...,vn} is contained in span(D)span(D). It can therefore, be a basis for DD. We will choose the set {v1,...,vn}{v1,...,vn} as the basis set. Here are the reasons.• These vectors minimize the average error due to projection• These vectors maximize the variance The above statements require more clarity and justification, which is given in the upcoming section.
5. PCA - Principal Component Analysis
L1L1L2L2 The red points are data points. There are two lines, L1L1 and L2L2. Minimizing Average Projection ErrorLet us ask the following question• Which line represent the data points better? L1
or
 L2L1orL2? Visually, it is apparent that L1L1 is better. The black line connecting a data point and L1L1 represents the perpendicular distance between the data point and L1L1. Similary, the blue line connecting a data point and L2L2 represents the perpendicular distance between the data point and L2L2. These lines represent the perpendicular distance. Which means, they also represent the error due to projection onto the corresponding lines. The blue lines are longer than the black lines. Therefore, the average error due to projection onto L1L1 is less than the average error due to projection onto L2L2. Let's formalize this idea. Consider the following dataset with mean 00. Each data point is a column vector.D={x1,...,xn}, X=a
―xT1―
―xT2―
⋮
―xTn―
D={x1,...,xn},X=[―x1T――x2T―⋮―xnT―] Every row in XX is a data point. A line is a span of a non-zero vector. Let's call it ww. The projection of a data point xixi onto span(w)span(w) is given by (xTiw)w
wTw(xiTw)wwTw. If ||w||=1||w||=1, then the projection is given by (xTiw)w(xiTw)w. The error due to projection is given by the vector xi-(xTiw)wxi-(xiTw)w. Goal : Among all the lines, we would like to find the line such that the avegrage projection error is mimimum. If that line is represented as spanaw1aspan(w^1), thenw1=
argmin

=1

nn∑i=1xTixi-(xTiw)21n∑i=1nxiTxi-(xiTw)2, the term xTixxiTx does not have ww. Therefore, it doesn't influence the optimal w1w^1. w1=

nXTX)w]w^1=argmaxw,st.||w||=1[wTCw]=argmaxw,st.||w||=1[wT(1nXTX)w] Let M=X
nM=Xn. Then, C=MTMC=MTM.w1=

w, st. ||w||=1[wTMTMw]=

w, st. ||w||=1[||Mw||2]w^1=argmaxw,st.||w||=1[wTMTMw]=argmaxw,st.||w||=1[||Mw||2] We have already solved a similar problem in section 22. w1w^1 is the eigenvector of MTM=CMTM=C corrsponding to the largest eigenvalue of CC. The eigenvector of CC corresponding to the largest eigenvalue of CC is the vector which minimizes the average projection error. Let's ask the same question, with slight variation::• Among all the lines that are orthogonal to w1w^1, which line minimizes the average projection error? Formally, w2w^2 is given as follows::w2=
argmin
w, st. ||w||=1&wT1w=0[1
nn∑i=1aaxi-(xTiw)waa2]w^2=argminw,st.||w||=1&w^1Tw=0[1n∑i=1n||xi-(xiTw)w||2] Once agian, we can simplify this to the following:w2=
argmin
w, st. ||w||=1&wT1w=0[1
nn∑i=1-(xTiw)2]=
argmax
w, st. ||w||=1&wT1w=0[wTMTMw]=
argmax
w, st. ||w||=1&wT1w=0[||Mw||2]w^2=argminw,st.||w||=1&w^1Tw=0[1n∑i=1n-(xiTw)2]=argmaxw,st.||w||=1&w^1Tw=0[wTMTMw]=argmaxw,st.||w||=1&w^1Tw=0[||Mw||2] A similar problem was solved in section 22. w2w^2 is the eigenvector of MTM=CMTM=C, corresponding to the second largest eigenvalue of CC. If rank(X)=krank(X)=k, then rank(C)=krank(C)=k. We can ask the same questions kk times. We will get kk eigenvectors corresponding to the top kk eigenvalues of CC. These eigenvalues will be greater than 00. These eigenvectors are reffered to as the principal components. Maximizing Variance
L1L1L2L2 Lets revist this image. We have already seen that L1L1 is better than L2L2, as the average error due to projection onto L1L1 is less than the average error due to projection onto L2L2. Lets shift the focus from the average error due to projection to the projections themseves.
L1L1L2L2 The black points are the projections. The projections onto L1L1 are widely "spread" across L1L1, whereas, the projections onto L2L2 are narrowly "spread" across L2L2. The data points are more oriented along direction of L1L1 when compared to L2L2. More the spread, better the line. How do we quantify "spread"? Answer : Variance. Each line can be expressed as the span of a unit vector. The projections onto that line is a scalar multiple of the unit vector. The norm of a unit vector is 11. Therefore, the scalar that multiplies the unit vector represents the length. Let ww represent a unit vector. The projection of a data point xixi onto span(w)span(w) is given by (xTiw)w(xiTw)w. Here, (xTiw)(xiTw) is the scalar. The line that best represents the data points shall be the line along which the variance of the projection is maximum. Let us denote the best line as span(w)span(w^). Then, w=
argmax
w, st. ||w||=1Var((xT1w), (xT2w), ..., (xTnw))w^=argmaxw,st.||w||=1Var((x1Tw),(x2Tw),...,(xnTw)) To find the variance, we would require the mean.
Mean((xT1w), (xT2w), ..., (xTnw))=1

w, st. ||w||=1[wTCw]w^=argmaxw,st.||w||=1[wTCw] We have already sloved this problem while minimizing the average error due to projection. The key insight is that, the line that minimizes the average projection error is the line that maximizes the variance. Let wiwi be the eigenvector of CC corresponding to the ithith largest eigenvalue (𝜆i)(𝜆i) of CC. Then,
wTiCwi=wTi(𝜆iwi)
=𝜆i(wTiwi)
=𝜆i
wiTCwi=wiT(𝜆iwi)=𝜆i(wiTwi)=𝜆i wTiCwiwiTCwi, which denotes the variance along wiwi is simplified to 𝜆i𝜆i. This implies that the valiance along wiwi is its corresponding eigenvalue. SummaryLet CC be the covariance matrix. w1,...,wkw1,...,wk are the eigenvectors of CC with 𝜆1,...,𝜆k𝜆1,...,𝜆k as the corresponding eigenvalues. Here, 𝜆1⩾𝜆2⩾...⩾𝜆k𝜆1⩾𝜆2⩾...⩾𝜆k Then, • w1w1 is the direction along which the variance of the projected data is maximum, and equivalently, it is also the direction along which the average projection error is mimium. The variance along w1w1 is 𝜆1𝜆1.• Among all the directions orthogonal to w1w1, w2w2 is the direction along which the variance of the projected data is maximum, and equivalently, it is also the direction along which the average projection error is mimium. The variance along w2w2 is 𝜆2𝜆2.• Among all the directions orthogonal to {w1,...,wk-1}{w1,...,wk-1}, wkwk is the direction along which the variance of the projected data is maximum, and equivalently, it is also the direction along which the average projection error is mimium. The variance along wkwk is 𝜆k𝜆k.
6. Mean-Centering the DatasetSo far, we have assumed that the mean of the data points in 00. In general, this may or may not be true.
L2L1 Structurally, the data points are aligned along L1L1. However, the line that minimizes the average projection error would look something similar to L2L2. This is because, the line that minimizes the average projection error must past through the origin. Here is a solution. First, we find the mean of the data points, Let's denote it as ⏨xx¯.⏨x=1
nn∑i=1xix¯=1n∑i=1nxi From every data point, we subtract the mean.xi=xi-⏨xxi=xi-x¯ Now, the mean of the data points will be 00. This process is known as "mean-centering" the dataset. After mean-centering the dataset shown in the above figure, the dataset would look as follows.
The line that minimizes the average error due to projection would also be the line that captures the structural alignment of the data points.
7. PCA in Higher DimensionsSuppose, we have nn data points in RdRd. If d>nd>n, computing C=1
nXTXC=1nXTX, which is a d×dd×d matrix, and finding its eigenvalues will be computationally costly. In this section, we shall look at a workaround for this. FACT : The non-zero eigenvalues of XTXXTX and XXTXXT are same. XXTXXT is an n×nn×n matrix. Let 𝜆1,...,𝜆k𝜆1,...,𝜆k be the kk non zero eigenvalues of C=1
nXTXC=1nXTX. Their corresponding eigenvectors are w1,...,wkw1,...,wk, where ||wi||=1||wi||=1, ∀i∈{1,...,k}∀i∈{1,...,k}. Here, 𝜆1⩾...⩾𝜆k𝜆1⩾...⩾𝜆k Let l1,...,lkl1,...,lk be the kk non zero eigenalues of XXTXXT. Their corresponding eigenvectors are 𝛽1,...,𝛽k𝛽1,...,𝛽k, where ||𝛽i||=1||𝛽i||=1, ∀i∈{1,...,k}∀i∈{1,...,k}. Here, l1⩾...⩾lkl1⩾...⩾lk Then,
